Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like I absolutely had to do something, then I'd binge through materials in long sessions. With 16 courses, I just couldn't keep up with everything in an organized way. I'd download stuff and work through it when I could, often late at night when my anxiety wasn't as overwhelming. It wasn't ideal, but I managed to get my assignments done somehow.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I never really had a set schedule—some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be up until 3am trying to catch up. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I know that's not great, but with my anxiety levels, sometimes just opening the LMS felt like too much.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or needing to submit something. Sometimes I'd get a notification that something was due soon and that would finally push me to log in. I rarely logged in just to browse or explore. It was always purpose-driven, like I needed to grab specific materials or check if I'd missed anything critical.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a habit for me—it always felt like a chore I had to force myself to do. The gamified tasks they introduced helped a little because they made things feel less heavy, and my mood improved when I used those. But even then, I wouldn't say it became routine. It was more like a tool I used when I absolutely needed it.
Q5: The video lectures were actually really helpful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could watch them, pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace. I watched a lot of video content—probably over four hours worth. The visual explanations stuck with me way better than reading dense text. When instructors used diagrams or demonstrated concepts visually, that's when things clicked.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time. I posted occasionally—maybe eleven times total—but I didn't really engage deeply with them. They felt performative, like people were just posting to get participation points. I also skipped a lot of the optional readings because I was already overwhelmed with the required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. The gamified tasks were actually motivating because they gave me small wins and made me feel like I was accomplishing something. That was nice when everything else felt so heavy and sad.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With so many courses and my mental state being what it is, I had to triage constantly. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones just fell off my radar. I always told myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. There's only so much energy I have.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded almost everything and studied offline constantly. I'd save PDFs, export slides, even screenshot important diagrams. Then I'd work through them on my tablet without being connected to anything. It was easier on my anxiety to study in my own space without feeling like I was being tracked or monitored.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened offline. I'd download a lecture video once, then rewatch it multiple times on my device. I'd print out notes and annotate them by hand. I'd spend hours reviewing materials that I'd only accessed once in the LMS. The system would show one download, but that represented maybe ten hours of actual study time.
Q11: All the time. That was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, grab everything I needed for a module, then disappear and work through it on my own terms. It felt safer that way, less pressure. I could take breaks when my anxiety spiked without worrying about session timeouts or losing my place.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd just freeze—I couldn't bring myself to log in or face any of it. I'd feel this overwhelming sadness and just... drift. Those were the weeks my attendance really dropped. I was still studying, just not in ways anyone could see.
Q13: The workload was brutal with 16 courses. I was constantly exhausted and had to make impossible choices about what to prioritize. My anxiety made everything harder—sometimes I'd spend an hour just trying to work up the courage to start. Fatigue was real; I'd fall asleep while watching lectures. The LMS became something I avoided when I was overwhelmed.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd be logged in, technically watching a video or scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be too anxious to actually absorb anything, or too sad to care. The activity was there, but the learning wasn't. It was like going through the motions without being present.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt intimidating or when my anxiety was bad. Even knowing something was important didn't always help—sometimes that made it worse because the stakes felt higher. I'd avoid discussion posts because putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt terrifying. My resilience just isn't great.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd check due dates and work backwards, cramming everything into the last possible window. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned. The LMS became most relevant to me in the 24-48 hours before something was due.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it, but my response time to engaging with feedback was slow—I'd often wait a day or so before I could emotionally handle looking at it. Good feedback lifted my mood and made me want to try harder. Critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for a while.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and requirements. Having everything laid out should have helped, but it just showed me how much I was behind. The gamified elements were a bright spot—they broke things into smaller, achievable pieces. But overall, the structure felt rigid and didn't accommodate my irregular engagement patterns.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because I could learn visually at my own pace. What made it harder was the emphasis on discussion participation and synchronous activities—those triggered my anxiety. Also, the sheer volume of content across 16 courses meant I was always playing catch-up. Simpler navigation would have helped too.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, downloading things, maybe watching videos, but struggling to actually complete and submit work. Or they could be anxious about their work not being good enough, so they keep reviewing materials but can't bring themselves to turn anything in. The gap between consuming content and producing work is real.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still got a Merit. I did most of my learning offline with downloaded materials. I completed almost all my assignments, just not through constant platform engagement. The LMS doesn't capture studying from printed notes at 2am or rewatching downloaded lectures on my tablet.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you were technically on a page. But they miss everything else—the offline studying, the mental state you were in, whether you actually understood anything. They can't measure if someone was anxiously staring at a screen without absorbing content, or deeply engaged with a printed handout.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not about how many times you log in—it's about whether the material is getting into your head and changing how you think. For me, engagement often happened in quiet moments alone with my notes, not in the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety or how much courage it took just to open certain assignments. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with offline materials, or how the gamified tasks actually improved my mood. It can't capture that I was sad and drifting through much of the semester, or that my engagement was real even when my presence on the platform wasn't.
